2605.00136.title: Are Tools All We Need? Unveiling the Tool-Use Tax in LLM Agents
2605.00136.paper_summary: Tool-augmented reasoning has become a popular direction for LLM-based agents, and it is widely assumed to improve reasoning and reliability. However, we demonstrate that this consensus does not always hold: in the presence of semantic distractors, tool-augmented reasoning does not necessarily outperform native CoT. To explain this performance gap, we propose a Factorized Intervention Framework that isolates the cost of prompt formatting, the overhead of the tool-calling protocol, and the actual gain from executing tools. Our analysis reveals a critical tradeoff: under semantic noise, the gains from tools often fail to offset the "tool-use tax", which is the performance degradation introduced by the tool-calling protocol itself. To address this, we introduce G-STEP, a lightweight inference-time gate to mitigate protocol-induced errors. While this yields partial recovery, our findings suggest that more substantial improvements still require strengthening the model's intrinsic reasoning and tool-interaction capabilities.
2605.00136.paper_summary_zh: 工具增強推理已成為基於大型語言模型（LLM）代理的一個熱門方向，並且廣泛認為能改善推理能力和可靠性。<lb/>然而，我們證明這一共識並不總是成立：在存在語義干擾的情況下，工具增強推理不一定優於原生的鏈式思考（CoT）。<lb/>為了解釋這一性能差距，我們提出了一個因子化干預框架，該框架將提示格式化的成本、工具調用協議的開銷以及執行工具的實際收益進行隔離。<lb/>我們的分析揭示了一個關鍵的權衡：在語義噪音下，工具所帶來的收益往往無法抵消“工具使用稅”，這是由工具調用協議本身引入的性能下降。<lb/>為了解決這個問題，我們引入了 G-STEP，一個輕量級的推理時閘門，用以減輕協議引起的錯誤。<lb/>雖然這帶來了部分恢復，但我們的研究結果表明，更實質的改進仍然需要加強模型的內在推理和工具互動能力。
2605.00136.author: Kaituo Zhang et.al.
2605.00136.authors: Kaituo Zhang, Zhen Xiong, Mingyu Zhong, Zhimeng Jiang, Zhouyuan Yuan, Zhecheng Li, Ying Lin
